author: @crypticnomad
text: I had an idea that might work as a sort of wind amplifier. The basic idea is that one of the ways that the wind is generated is by temperature differences or rather air pressure can be increased by using a temperature differential. To be a bit more specific I'm thinking that the processes of convection and/or advection could be used to sort of amplify the wind. If we have a hot sand chamber and cold water chamber with some sort of connection between the two there should be a wind formed from the cold chamber to the warm chamber or rather increase air pressure between the two. So I was thinking maybe having a chamber painted black and a reflective chamber, a connection between the two, and the turbine sitting on the outlet of the warm chamber or maybe some other point like where the cold air meets the warm air. Then if we funnel the air from this device into the cold chamber maybe the temperature differences would increase the air pressure. Using something like a sand battery combined with something like the clay pot condensation cooling method could possibly help maintain the extra push even after the sun goes down. Tornados are formed when there is warm moist air near the ground, cool dry air above it and wind shear. Maybe those conditions could be reproduced somehow?

author: @Simon_Rafferty
text:
Static torque (Moment) or open circuit voltage tell you nothing useful!
Power is proportional to Torque x Angular velocity. 
The easiest (useful) option is to measure the current flow and voltage across a fixed resistance - which will give you the power, albeit with the generator efficiency wrapped up in the value.
That would be fine for comparitive measurements,  using the same generator - which I think is what you're trying to achieve.

The value of the resistor will change as it heats up - which will affect your measurement.

You could either map the change in resistance with temperature, measure its temperature and wrap that into the power calculation,  or use a mig enough heat-sync that the temperature will barely change.  A kettle is a reasonable option for this. Start with the same volume of water at the same temperature at the start of each run.

author: @giles1962
text:
Rather than having the rear shield, which would need to rotate in-line with the wind direction, could you add vanes to the cones?  These vertical vanes would stop the wind going straight through the cones and then divert the wind in either an up, or down direction.  That way the unit would be omni directional.
I think a down wind direction may be best, that way the turbine, with the blades, magnets and coils could all be located closer to the ground, or it’s attachment point.
One last thing, maybe a rotating shield could also be used, after all.  However, instead of behind the cones, I would locate the shield at the wind outlet side, and then in the opposite direction.  In this configuration the shield would block the oncoming wind and act like a venturi (creating a lower air pressure behind the turbine), this should then actually suck the wind out of that area, adding more power to the unit.

author: @derekiwi
text: What you say about speed and voltage not being an indicator of actual power not really correct, because it is power you are looking for. To get an more real measurement of torque and therefore power you could put a low’ish value resistor on the output and then measure voltage, because it is doing some work.

author: @recless8667
text:
I understand that putting all the electrics on the bottom keeps you from clambering up the thing, but you also need to consider how you're going to *remove* said electrics. With your current implementation, you have to remove the entire wind capture device to remove the generator etc.

An access hatch would be a good idea, as would inverting the device. If your wind capture shrouds end up weighing in excess of 100lbs when they're finally made out of steel, you have quite a problem brewing when you eventually need to remove it to service the generator. You also need to consider a lockout / tagout mechanism so you can safely access the electrics for service without losing a hand to the spinning parts, or getting your body pulled in from an unbuttoned shirt, long hair, beard, etc.

author: @bodymindresiliencechannel
text: Another question I have is - at what point does this 0.1mm wire burn out?   I've done a few calculations for power of air, and if the inlet is very efficient, the power output in 50mph winds using a (say) 600x600 Darwin is likely to go past the current capacity of the wire.  It's a bit frustrating.  So I'm tempted to increase the diameter of the impellor slightly, which will then potentially increase the generated power capacity, but also give room for a 0.3mm winding instead of a 0.1mm winding.  Lots of numbers to jiggle, and at some point I have to stop running it through my head and actually build something.  I'm starting to get the point that a DIY system of this type is almost inevitably going to fail somewhere, and it's a very different mindset from just buying something off the shelf.

author: @bodymindresiliencechannel
text: Yes - really interesting.  I've been having a think about the collector system, because it seems that this is the most important part... The idea is that wind energy (pressure-volume) from a large surface area is crammed into a small pipe.  I would guess that the fan itself when rotating will help clear this pressure-volume, but there is inevitably going to be a back-pressure - so how can that be reduced?  Right at the start I'd say that the set of circular holes is not enough free exhaust area and will generate a back-pressure.  One way of installing this in my house is to put the collector at the top of a single storey extension and the generator at the bottom with a 160mm x 3m soil pipe joining the two.  How can I maximise the pressure differential and the volume of air entering?  One possibility is to add a turbocharger.  A small spiral ugrinsky vawt could sit (upside down) at the top attached to a ducted axial fan, pushing air down the top/middle cone.  If the cones are pseudospheres, then the middle cone can also be slightly lengthened and act as a venturi, which would then draw more air in through the side orifices.  As someone said below - it looks like a jet engine.  It's also possible to go completely crazy with this and also add a cowl and an extraction turbo on the outlet (another Ugrinsky driving another ducted axial fan) ... so there is a fan-assisted positive pressure on the inlet and a fan-assisted vacuum on the outlet.  Way too complex.

replies.author: @MarcoTheHammer
replies.text:
I'm currently living off grid in my tiny RV. Currently, it’s parked in the equipment storage yard of the construction company that I work for. Allow me to comment on your proposed modifications...

1. I'm not certain a smooth surface is the best treatment for this situation. It makes sense but consider the effects of the dimples on a golf ball as well as the wind tunnel studies by NASCAR racers in the '60s that brought vinyl tops to the automotive industry.
Perhaps some spiral-like veins in the ‘funnel’ area could improve airflow and get it headed in the right direction?

2. This is a great idea at first glance! We would have to consider the potential of the power drag and calculate it into the final equation, but I imagine you already have this in mind...

3. I concur! This was the first thing to come to my mind when near the end of the video Robert Murray-Smith asked viewers for suggestions about improvements. Perhaps fewer vertical supports could be used if designed with the most aerodynamic friendly specs or simply changing the shape of the exhaust ports?
I live about 35 miles South of Seattle WA and we don't get as much usable sunshine as other areas so the idea of mini wind powered generators is attractive to me. Maybe several small units could be ganged together for better performance than a single large unit? Some individual units could also be able to continue generating power if one unit was to fail temporarily.
I use a ‘SunGoldPower’ brand 3Kw inverter/charger/PV controller without any solar panels up yet. I have two 24-volt 320-watt Renogy solar panels standing by, but I know I’ll need several more to collect the 2.4K watts that I typically use daily. 
Currently, I have two 24-volt, 100 Ah ‘Chins’ brand lithium batteries that I take down to about 50% daily to maximize their lifespan and I re-charge them to 100% daily. I’m happy with their performance so far. The ‘Chins’ recommended charging rate is limited to 40 amps. I have a Westinghouse dual fuel I use a 3650-watt propane fueled ICE generator with wireless remote control that makes using it during our longer than average winter and wet weather easier than going to start it outside. It takes about 2.5 hours to re-charge the batteries and so far the cost of propane fuel (without road taxes!) has been reasonable.

author: @tomscott1159
text: For many years, sailors have used a wind catcher to funnel air down through a square deck hatch. If you sew a triangular fabric panel with a base width about 2x the hatch opening width, then pull the apex up with a halyard and attach the base corners to two adjacent hatchway corners, you have an efficient catcher for wind which blows over the catcher-attached side of the hatch. If you replicate the panel with another, attach its base to the two opposite corners of the hatchway, the apex to the same halyard, and then sew the two panels together along a vertical line to form an "x" when looking up through the hatchway, then you have a catcher which works fairly well in all directions. A sailmaker could help you shape the panels to encourage efficient vertical airflow.

Now, I know you're not trying to make this thing take off, however, if you modified the vents around the bottom of your shroud such that they were rings running around the whole outside edge which directed the air downwards, the air could be directed down onto the flying saucer shape.

author: @magic.marmot
text:
Part of the transfer is the impedance matching. The larger input has a lower impedance to air pressure, and translates to a higher impedance to increase the force transfer to the mechanical turbine itself. It's pretty much the opposite of a horn driver in audio systems. 
There is always an entropy loss in impedance matching, but considering the purpose, it's an excellent design.

author: @magic.marmot
text:
I really like your research and building this as a prototype. 
I'm a bit disappointed in the shroud test because the air source is lower with the shroud on than with the shroud off, and more of the energy of the air source is going to be able to transfer to mechanical energy just because of the reduction of turbulence. 
I do believe that there is an increase, I don't believe it's actually twice as much because there will always be boundary effect losses. 
Also, I think part of the reason that the commercial unit is upside-down from your configuration is that if you were to mount the device on a peaked roof of a normal house, any horizontal wind would gain a vortical vector that could be additive to the energy transfer. 
I still haven't figured out roof peak dynamics, though I know that the windward side has a more laminar flow than the leeward side, which breaks into chaotic turbulence and loses energy.

author: @shveylien7401
text: I was thinking about your vertical sections and hex vs oct. Dec is the answer. With 10 sides the inner vertical wall sections will have broad enough coverage and approach angle to maximize air deflection and collection. I like the cheese wedge design to ensure directional flow, but remember the cross section of air volume is what you need to calculate for generator diameter, and the drag is real in smaller pipes. Airgun people know there's a limit to how fast you can push air down a .177 barrel vs a .22 barrel. Laminar flow layers compound drag exponentially in narrow diameter pipes. You might want to ensure the vortex/ventrui effect is in rotation with your generator with some impellers or some surface guides. Your shroud shouldn't be wings out of a tube, it should be a smooth W shape to pull side air into the back sections and it should be twice as wide as the unit if structure will permit in winds, perhaps with a missing center section for 360 degree first wind approach. I also suggest not a solid top cap, but a 35 degree slat roof in cheese wedge orientation, flat or slanted or domed. Wouldn't it be nice to have both tech, the Airomine airfoil shape causing vacuum to draw on the pipe which the newton collector and generator are in and maximize potentials on both ends of the same pipe?

author: @PhilWare1
text: Surely generator efficiency will vary with rotational speed? Unless you have an accurate calibration (i.e. Efficiency vs speed at different electrical loads) for your home-made generator then your relative voltage readings will not be directly comparable. I'm not saying that your method for using voltage increase as a proxy for torque is wrong, its just that you will have to account for this additional factor first. Interestingly, the most efficient generators run at a FIXED speed in order to target this sweet-spot as is the case for most wind-turbines.

author: @dariusj33
text:
One drawback of having the mechanics at the bottom of this "mushroom" is that you'll have to maintain it more. Leaves, dust, ice cream papers and plastic bags and other junk tends to move downwards due to gravity. Sure, the wind momentum will smash them at the turbine even when it's at the top, but then when the wind gust passes and the turbine stops, the rubbish is more likely to fall off it, i.e. a turbine that's at the top has a self-cleaning property.

Another benefit of having it at the top is better contact with the ground/better (theoretical) stability. Because of drag forces. Your "mushroom" is pointing downwards, acting as a blade trying to lift the whole thing.

I definitely agree with you, maintaining the generator when it's at the bottom is definitely easier. But that may also mean it's more likely to _need_ maintenance.

replies.author: @Bennyboy-dog
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering The generator just converts one form of energy to another - mechanical energy to electrical energy, and since they will do that at 85% - 95% efficiency at any speed the mechanicals can tolerate,  the current generated will be the torque times some constant. The mppt controller merely removes the need to find the right load for any specific rpm by fixing the output voltage regardless of input voltage then the only thing that will vary with the torque will be the current out of the mppt controller into a fixed load.

 In short, turn the holes into vertical rectangles that match the same hight as the rotor, more flow in = more power out.    A way to improve the incoming flow of air  would be to take a closer look at the flow going out 👍
 And while improvements are going on, change the flat spots that will be between the vertical rectangles into triangles and make them as thin as possible to reduce escaping wind turbulence even more 👍

Sorry for the novel, I just get excited when my minds eye is opened,  keep up the great work..

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
7:15 SUGGESTION: EXTRACT THE CONES FROM SHEETS OF THERMOPLASTIC OVER A MOLD.

Describe the superheterodyne [or, if you prefer, the 'anti-heterodyne'] in Tinker-cad, or with a compass and a sheet of paper, cut out a piece of wood of that shape, bend some stuff wire [3 to 4 mm should be quite sufficient, but if you do a lot of them, even coat hanger wire could be made to work], solder them all into a 'lamp shade' of sorts, [or even find a bunch of lamp shades all the right size and angle cheaply from some wholesale company] and then stretch a sheet of 4 to 6 mil HDPE plastic film over a 'picture-frame-clamping jig, cut the center hole out and line it with tape before stretching to prevent the plastic from tearing, then with just a little gentle heat [less than 80C] stretch each wire frame through the plastic, allow it to cool, then tape the outer edge to keep it from fraying in the wind.

I suppose, with a little bit of care, one could even modify common umbrellas to do this work, as they are quite cheap, and might possibly be usable with a little reconstruction of their spokes, along with them allowing one to have a common shaft, by putting the pointy tip of one piece up into the cut open piece of the next one in line [like stackable drink cups] and perhaps some hot glue to hold them together, and then this can sit on a wire, builder's board, wood, or printed plastic support on each end to keep it all in a line and keep it at the right distance.

That funnel need not be anything extremely thick or heavy, since all it is doing is deflecting air, so long as its frame is secure and stable.

author: @p739-n2i
text: Forget torque.  KW = volts x amps  Volts are equivalent /proportional to speed and amps are equivalent /proportional  to torque.  Put a constant load on your generator.  Use the same generator with a constant load (resistance),  same wind device at the same distance. If you increase the efficiency of the turbine your volts and amps will increase thereby showing an increase in speed and torque.  Your measurement will also be actual useable output. Measuring this at different wind speeds will show weakness and or strengths. You can also measure speed with a non contact tachometer or by measuring the AC frequency.

author: @bartronicsecurity
text: Now you are doing the real stuff Rob. To help me get a better understanding of things I always like using mental pictures. And one way to do that is to equate mechanical RPM to electrical volts  and mechanical torque with electical amps. You need rpm x torque for mechanical power just like you need volts x amps for electrical power. So in your setup where you have a tight mechanical -electrical coupling you have to look at the big picture. You could get high rpm which results in a high volts reading but the high rpm with no torque will also result in no mechanical power which will result in no usable current / electrical power. If you do load the electrical output it will result in the mechanical section slowing down possibly to a near stop if heavily loaded on the elctrical side. So what I do in my developments is have a grossly over sized electrical generator (maybe 5 times the mechanical side in power potential)  which could stop the mechanical side if the  heavily loaded. I then set the wind / fan up at a given wind speed and do a series volt to amps graph readings with a varying resistive load on the electrical side. In this way I get the MPP of the mechanical wind side. Then I do a change to the wind / mechanical side and retest the MPP and see if the power has improved for the same wind speed. Once I am reading a good bit of power on the electrical side I know the mechanical side is good and now start working on the electrical side. I do changes in the generator side  to see if I can get more electrical power (using graphs and MPP) after each change with a given set uo of wind speed and mechnical setup. Takes a long time but you get to learn a lot with each change / addition.

author: @Xero1of1
text:
I just had a thought... and maybe I'm splitting hairs or being cheeky... But... It occurs to me that there be another avenue of torque increase you could incorporate into your design. Assuming your wind catcher will be up on a pipe to collect the faster moving wind, all of the wind blowing against the pipe will simply slide around either end, right? What if you put a couple fins on the pipe section to have that lower, uncollected wind spin the pipe as well? Your collector at the top will still direct the air downwards, but it'll add a slight spin to the air as it goes down. Then, with the pipe spinning and the stator connected to the base of the spinning pipe, if the rotor spins contrary to the pipe, then you'd be adding the rotation of the pipe and stator to the rotation of the rotor... More RPMs. You should still have the same amount of wind coming down the pipe at roughly the same speed and pressure... after all, you're still catching the same amount of wind. 

I'm not sure how the slight spin of the air as it's being caught and directed down a spinning pipe would affect the overall outcome, but I think you'd be able to utilize some of the otherwise wasted wind to some degree.

author: @hughjardon3538
text: Torque is directly proportional to current in a DC motor or generator. Voltage means nothing, though you can use it to calculate power when you know the current. To get the current, you must load the generator to make it slow down. There will be a speed under load that gives maximum power. You must apply different loads to see what that is.

replies.author: @feras5017
replies.text: A variable resistor may work? And the fan should not be held by hand, it shkuld be fixed in a position to make sure that you have same angle of attack and distance between the fan and the generator.

replies.author: @hughjardon3538
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering The only thing you need from a generator is watts. The question is how many can you get at typical wind speed. For that, you can simulate the wind with a blower, but then you must load the generator so that you know how much current it can make at that wind speed, then you can measure the volts at that current and calculate the watts, or connect one of those cheap Ebay watt-meters that give you it directly. 

When you load a generator, the torque to turn it increases with the current, so it slows down.

author: @Cee64E
text: Something I remember from my Army days:  When testing our tank's engine during quarterly services, one test that was done is how much torque the engine produced.  These were obviously very large engines, weighing about 2.5 tons, so not easy to put it on a Dynamo.  What would be done is to measure the engine's _acceleration_ from idle to redline.  Since the engine's rotating mass was known, how fast that mass could be accelerated was a good measure of the engine's power.  Perhaps you could do something similar.  Using a measured wind speed, see how fast the turbine goes from 0 RPM to some fixed limit.  The mass of the turbine's rotating components is known, as is the wind speed.  You just need the right equation.  I'm terrible at math, so you're on your own there.

All you need to do is feed the first one!
That's a "Bernoulli Venturi Stack"!
As air flows past each Venturi, they will ALL draw in air on all sides!
Same principle as a spray gun!
Actually shielding the lower portion of the stack and only directly feeding the first one would surprisingly increase efficiency!
Letting the wind hit the entire stack will disrupt the effect!
Airflow out the bottom will be more consistent, and directional!

author: @danedmiston9673
text: Speed as a Tip Speed Ratio ( the speed on the outer tip of the blade divided by the wind speed) is very useful.  Using Hertz readings instead of volts from the alternator the actual RPM can be calculated from the number of magnets in the alternator.  Using the RPM and the circumference of the rotor the Tip Speed is calculated.  Divide the no load Tip Speed by the wind speed to get the turbines TSR  (Tip Speed Ratio).  In general the higher the TSR the better the efficiency.  Most wind turbines generate the most power when loaded to run at 1/2 the no load TSR.

author: @HP3.14
text:
All wind turbines have one thing in common: they prefer laminar flow.
Either you place the turbine at high altitude or you create laminar flow.
Torque is a function of force related to the radius of the rotating shaft.
T=r*F
So you can increase the torque by increasing the diameter of the turbine or increasing the force acting on it.
If you want to test different turbine designs, you have to keep several things constant. The force acting on the turbine (wind speed) and the diameter of the turbine. Just a thought.

author: @johnbarry8185
text:
Robert, create a plexiglass cylinder to go anterior to the  Water's turbine.  Then place a constant flow of air into the cylinder.  Use either smoke or colored air, to see where the air flow is NOT moving through the turbine.  From there, you can improve the turbine.  It is my belief, that there will be quite a bit of turbulence at the top of the turbine, that is escaping WITHOUT moving through the fins of the squirrel cage.  This will be the inefficiency of the turbine.  You would probably do better, to make the squirrel cage the same shape as the cone, only slightly larger, to allow for the air to travel in, and have no way out, except through the fins.
Keep up the good work.

replies.text: Not only that, but I think their decision was also based on avoiding water "collection" towards the bottom of the unit. I think ventum dynamics are onto something "big" - and Robert as well. Ventum is working on a "home" "Ikea style" version - but an open source alternative is always great! :)

replies.author: @matheusfaria7230
replies.text:
@geoe5621 Yes, when I first started watching the video and the machine was upside down I thought it was just a cool normal project but when I saw that picture showing that it's actually the other way around it instantly triggered my interest. I do believe that even if his or their design doesn't work the concept itself is really future-seeking and things should be developed trying to follow it... 

Turning air energy which is currently something "temperamental" into something stable and predictable, even if extremely less powerful, would already change the world. Another huge factor is that this type of design doesn't seem to disrupt the enviroment and also wouldn't need the costful maintenance that current turbines do (that's their major deterrent)... Make it work and give them a magnetic bearing and you have the next-gen power plant.
